segments.text: Here is a horrible statistic.
71% of people perceive meetings as unproductive and boring.
In this video, I want to give you a couple of ideas
to shake up your meetings
to make them a little more fun and engaging.
I would recommend setting aside at least one meeting a month
where you do something different and fun.
This will help engagement.
It will also help your colleagues and your teammates
get to know each other better.
And also these ideas are so great.
We're just getting a little mental recharge
and creative break.
Idea number one.
Bring in your favorite childhood snack.
You can even rotate who brings in what?
So July, someone brings in their favorite Twinkie.
In August, someone brings in their favorite hot dog
where you have a cool snack
and you don't know what it's going to be
until you show up to the meeting.
Idea number two.
Unique skill demo.
You probably know your colleagues,
skills, and talents at work.
But do you know their skills and talents outside of work?
Have one meeting a month where you rotate
and someone shows off a unique skill.
That could be playing the flute or making sushi
or a superior knowledge of cartoons from the 50s.
Whatever that is, they can develop a little PowerPoint
or do an activity where they get to show off that skill
for just a few minutes at the start or end of a meeting.
Three, I love this one.
Art Walk.
This is great if you're also in a virtual team.
Art Walk is you show people a piece of art in your house
and you explain what it meant to you.
It could be a movie poster or a kids drawing
or a banner that you bought on your trip to Switzerland,
any kind of art that's in your house.
You can show it off and talk about what it means
to you on your video call.
This is a really great way to spice up
the beginning of a virtual meeting
when everyone takes turns showing a piece of art
in their house.
By the way, if people don't have art,
you could also do pet show off or kid show off,
film the blank for whatever you wanna show people
in your house that has a cool story.
Idea number four.
Simple but easy, outdoor meeting.
You can do this in person
where you do the entire meeting outside
or even if you're virtual,
encourage everyone to find a good outdoor spot
and do the meeting wherever they are outdoors.
Five, this is a super simple idea.
You could do it at the start of every meeting.
You pick a warm up song.
So you rotate who gets to pick it,
but at the very beginning of the meeting,
when everyone's first locking on,
someone gets to pick some kind of warm up song.
It could be their favorite song or a childhood song
and they get to briefly explain why they picked it.
It's great for those first minute or two
when people are slowly logging on and you're saying,
"We'll wait for people to get online."
And you're just checking your email.
It's better to listen to a song altogether.
Six, office scavenger hunt.
Okay, I love this one.
It's super fun.
You could also do a city scavenger hunt,
depending on how you're doing.
You don't have to make these big and extravagant.
You could even do one hunt after each meeting
where there's a secret clue in the office
for whoever wants to find it.
This is great if you're a little bit creative
and you just wanna get people excited
for something at the end of the meeting,
you could have a secret clue waiting for them.
Number seven, be a kid meeting, do finger painting,
try finger foods, make paper airplanes together.
This can be a five or 10 minute activity.
It doesn't have to take the entire meeting,
but it will shake things up
and hopefully get creative juices flowing.
This is especially good to do
before a creative collaboration meeting
or a brainstorm meeting.
Eight, show and tell.
I loved show and tell as a kid.
People can bring in any object they want
and talk about it in their meeting.
You can do this virtually or in person.
It's so fun to do this at least once a year.
Nine, team book club.
So team book club is great
because the team can pick a book that they're reading,
assign the book, get everyone a month or two to read it.
And then you could have a team meeting
where you discuss the book, talk about questions
and that way you're learning and growing together.
Last one, bring in a speaker, bring in me.
I love coming to lunch and learning meetings.
Is there a local speaker or someone that you think
would help your team level up with professional development,
reach out to them, reach out to me,
see if I can come and speak to your meeting.
I'm in Austin, Texas, if anyone's local,
and see if you can learn from a speaker or an expert.
If you don't want to bring in a speaker,
you can also just watch a great TED Talk.
Watch my TED Talk at the start of a meeting
or watch a TED Talk together virtually
so that it's bringing an expert
can also bring in fresh perspective.
(upbeat music)
